text:
The British embassy in Dubai issued the statement on Friday, a day after a British legal assistance group, Detained in Dubai, said that at least 21 people had been charged with committing offenses by taking or sharing videos and photos of missile strikes and damage.
The group did not disclose the nationalities of those it said had been charged, and The New York Times could not independently verify the cases.
The Emirati foreign ministry and the Government of Dubai media office did not respond to requests for information.
Britain’s Foreign Office confirmed on Friday that it was in contact with authorities in the Emirates over the detention of a 60-year-old British man, who has not been named.
In a statement, it urged foreign citizens living in the country to also be aware of local laws that it said made it “illegal to post material, including videos and photographs, online that is critical of the U.A.E. government, companies or individuals, or which relates to incidents in the U.A.E.”
title: Britain warns its citizens in Dubai they could be jailed for sharing photos of damage from airstrikes.

citability.title: Britain warns its citizens in Dubai they could be jailed for sharing photos of damage from airstrikes.
